questions.scenario: DevPolaris is releasing a new orders API image to an ECS service. The image built successfully and the service update created two replacement tasks, but neither task passed the target-group health check. Customers now receive `503` responses from the ALB, and both replacement tasks log that required `DATABASE_URL` configuration is missing. The release captain must restore service without treating a successful image build as proof that the runtime is ready. What should the captain do before allowing this release to continue?

options.text: Treat the image as complete release evidence and investigate only target-group configuration because the service update succeeded.
Treat missing `DATABASE_URL` as an application defect because the task reached RUNNING before health checks failed.
Treat the release as blocked, restore the required database configuration and its runtime access path, redeploy, and require healthy targets before continuing.
Treat the 503 as a capacity problem and increase desired count before checking why each replacement task lacks configuration.

questions.explanation:
The built image and updated service prove only part of the release path. Both tasks identify the same missing runtime dependency and fail readiness, so adding capacity or changing the target group would reproduce the failure. The release should remain blocked until configuration, secret access, task startup, and target health all satisfy the runtime contract.

Learn more in [Amazon ECS task definitions](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/task_definitions.html).

questions.scenario: DevPolaris has deployed its staging orders API on an EC2 instance in a private subnet behind an internet-facing Application Load Balancer. The systemd service is active, and `curl http://127.0.0.1:3000/health` returns `200` on the instance. However, the target group reports `Target.Timeout` for `i-0abc123:3000`. The instance security group currently allows TCP 3000 only from the administration bastion security group, while production traffic must arrive through the ALB security group. What is the most appropriate change?

options.text: Change the target group health path because local `/health` proves the listener can reach port 3000.
Attach a public IPv4 address to the instance so the internet-facing ALB can reach the target directly.
Allow TCP 3000 from the VPC CIDR to restore reachability while preserving a private target.
Allow the ALB security group to reach the instance security group on port 3000, then recheck target health.

questions.explanation:
The local service is healthy, but the ALB cannot reach it. The security group evidence shows port 3000 is allowed from the bastion, not from the ALB. That makes the network path a better first fix than code or disk changes.

Learn more in [VPC security groups](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc/latest/userguide/vpc-security-groups.html).

questions.scenario: An ECS release creates RUNNING tasks, but customers receive 503 responses and both targets fail with `Target.ResponseCodeMismatch`. The application log confirms that `/health` returns 500 because `DATABASE_URL` is absent. The prior task definition references the database secret in its container `secrets` field, while the new revision has no `DATABASE_URL` entry. Which correction best follows the evidence?

options.text: Restore the reviewed `DATABASE_URL` secret reference in the task definition, deploy replacement tasks, and require healthy targets before continuing.
Add HTTP 500 to the target group's success-code matcher so the release can serve traffic while configuration is repaired.
Grant `secretsmanager:GetSecretValue` to the application task role even though the application never makes a runtime Secrets Manager call.
Increase desired count because additional tasks can compensate for each task receiving the same incomplete definition.

questions.explanation:
The new task definition omits the secret reference that existed in the reviewed revision, so every replacement task receives the same incomplete runtime contract. Masking readiness failure or adding capacity would send traffic to broken tasks. If ECS later reports a pre-start secret retrieval denial, the task execution role becomes relevant; this evidence instead shows a missing task-definition entry.

Learn more in [Passing Secrets Manager secrets to ECS containers](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/secrets-envvar-secrets-manager.html).

questions.scenario: DevPolaris is consolidating 35 containerized services from two business units. The platform team already operates Kubernetes controllers, admission policies, Helm releases, and shared observability, and product teams require Kubernetes APIs for custom rollout and scheduling behavior. Leadership accepts the control-plane and cluster-operating cost but wants a managed AWS control plane. Which three facts support evaluating Amazon EKS rather than choosing it merely because the workloads use containers?

options.text: The platform team already owns Kubernetes operations and can support cluster upgrades, policy, capacity, and workload troubleshooting.
EKS removes responsibility for worker capacity, add-ons, networking, and Kubernetes version upgrades.
The workloads require Kubernetes APIs and controllers that are part of the existing delivery and governance model.
A shared Kubernetes platform can justify its operational cost across many services with consistent platform needs.
Any containerized service should use EKS once the number of services exceeds one.

questions.explanation:
EKS is justified by Kubernetes-specific requirements plus a team and service portfolio that can carry the operating model. AWS manages the control plane, but the team still owns worker capacity, networking, add-ons, policy, upgrades, and workload diagnosis. Container packaging alone does not require Kubernetes; ECS with Fargate may be simpler when those APIs and platform conventions are not needed.

Learn more in [Amazon EKS concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/what-is-eks.html).

questions.scenario:
DevPolaris is separating receipt email from the checkout request. The proposed worker starts from SQS, usually runs for less than 700 ms, does not listen on a port, and must prevent duplicate sends with an idempotency key.

Name is receipt-email-worker. Starts when is SQS message arrives. Work duration is usually under 700 ms. Needs port listener is no. Duplicate risk is must use idempotency key receipt:{orderId}.

Which compute choice fits best?

options.text: Use a small Fargate worker with desired count one so the team can reuse its container deployment and logging model.
Lambda, because the job is event-shaped and should be safe across retries.
Run the email call in the checkout ECS task to avoid operating another asynchronous runtime.
Use an EC2 worker because the team wants full control over retry and concurrency behavior.

questions.explanation:
The trigger and duration point to Lambda. The idempotency note is also important because queue-based work can retry. The main checkout service should not wait on slow side effects if a queue can decouple them.

Learn more in [AWS Lambda concepts](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda/latest/dg/welcome.html).

questions.scenario:
DevPolaris finance needs a monthly export from the production orders database. The job must process 14 million rows, checkpoint progress, and finish outside the current Lambda time limit.

Trigger is monthly schedule. Work is scans 14 million rows, can run 45 minutes. Partial progress is should checkpoint and resume. Timeout requirement is longer than current Lambda limit and needs steady worker logs.

What should the team consider?

options.text: Keep one Lambda invocation for the 45-minute scan and raise its configured timeout to match the job.
Run the export inside a continuously serving ECS API task so it can reuse the checkout database pool.
Schedule the job on the existing checkout EC2 host so no separate worker runtime is required.
Use a longer-running worker shape such as ECS or EC2 instead of forcing the job into Lambda.

questions.explanation:
Event-shaped does not automatically mean Lambda. The path asks whether the work can finish inside function limits and whether the operational shape fits. A long checkpointed job may be cleaner as a worker service.

Learn more in [AWS Fargate for Amazon ECS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/AWS_Fargate.html).

questions.scenario:
Six months later, a teammate wants to move the receipt worker from Lambda to the main ECS service. Current evidence says:

Queue age is normal. Duplicate sends is 0 after idempotency fix. Function errors is low. Checkout latency is stable because email is off the request path.

How should the old decision record guide the review?

options.text: Use the record to ask what problem the move solves and what tradeoff changes, rather than changing compute just for uniformity.
Keep Lambda unless the old record explicitly predicted this migration, even if new operational evidence changes the tradeoff.
Move to ECS to reduce the number of deployment mechanisms, without identifying a reliability, cost, or ownership problem.
Compare only current compute cost because queue behavior and failure handling stay the same after a runtime migration.

questions.explanation:
A decision record is not a cage. It is a memory aid for tradeoffs. If current evidence is healthy, the reviewer should ask what new requirement justifies the move and what operational cost it introduces.

Learn more in [AWS Fargate for Amazon ECS](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/AWS_Fargate.html).
